serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of stir-fried bitter gourd at an economic rice stall is approximately 100 g, representing one scoop placed alongside rice and other dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Bittergourd — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F14080034 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken from this canonical HPB entry.

gi_source.title: Bitter melon / bitter gourd — analogous GI estimate based on non-starchy vegetable reference data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for stir-fried bitter gourd exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Bitter gourd is a non-starchy vegetable with 3.9 g carbohydrate and 2.97 g fibre per 100 g. A GI of approximately 32 is assigned by analogy with other low-GI non-starchy vegetables (e.g., green beans GI ~30, mixed vegetables GI ~35) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (32 × 3.9) / 100 = 1.25, rounded to 1.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 330 mg per 100 g from added soy sauce and salt
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 80–100 g and request reduced soy sauce and salt at the stall to keep sodium intake below 400 mg per serving; the dish's natural potassium (~200 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (2.97 g/100 g) partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (3.9 g/100 g), high fibre (2.97 g/100 g), and bitter gourd's documented hypoglycaemic bioactives (charantin, polypeptide-p)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried bitter gourd is an excellent choice for Type 2 Diabetes management given its low glycemic load (~1.2 per 100 g serving), high fibre content, and evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties; patients on insulin or secretagogues should monitor blood glucose as additive hypoglycaemic effects are possible.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.5 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (2.97 g/100 g), and antioxidant-rich bitter gourd
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, making it cardioprotective; however, the 330 mg sodium per 100 g from seasoning should be moderated by requesting less soy sauce to keep total sodium within cardiovascular-friendly limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High vitamin C (~40 mg/100 g) and antioxidants reduce oxidative stress; no pro-inflammatory components
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Bitter gourd's vitamin C and antioxidant content support anti-inflammatory benefits for rheumatoid arthritis; reducing added soy sauce and salt to keep sodium below 400 mg/100 g will further minimise risk of fluid retention and tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Bitter Gourd (Economic Rice) is a classic Singaporean hawker side dish served at economic rice (cai fan) stalls, where diners select individual dishes to accompany steamed white rice. The dish consists of sliced bitter gourd (Momordica charantia) stir-fried in vegetable oil with aromatics such as garlic, seasoned with soy sauce and salt until fragrant and just tender. The result is a slightly bitter, savoury vegetable dish with a firm yet yielding texture. Nutritionally, it is low in calories (46 kcal/100 g), low in carbohydrates (3.9 g/100 g), and rich in dietary fibre (2.97 g/100 g), making it one of the most diabetes-friendly options at an economic rice stall. Bitter gourd contains charantin and polypeptide-p, bioactive compounds with documented hypoglycaemic properties. Sodium is moderate at 330 mg/100 g, primarily from seasoning. The dish is naturally vegetarian, vegan, and halal. It is a staple working-lunch choice across Singapore's multicultural population and is particularly valued for its health benefits in managing blood glucose and supporting digestive health.
common_user_queries: Is stir-fried bitter gourd good for diabetes?
How many calories in bitter gourd economic rice?
Is bitter gourd high in sodium?
What is the GI of bitter gourd?
Can I eat bitter gourd if I have hypertension?
What is cai fan bitter gourd?

confidence_reason: Core macros and micronutrients are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14080034, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from a peer-reviewed source (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) as no direct GI measurement exists for this preparation; this reduces GI confidence to medium but does not affect overall record confidence given the strong nutritional provenance.
notes: 1. Saturated fat estimated at 0.5 g/100 g based on typical vegetable oil stir-fry preparation; HPB Foodo does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. 2. Potassium estimated at ~200 mg/100 g and vitamin C at ~40 mg/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central data for raw bitter gourd (USDA SR Legacy item 11091), adjusted for cooking losses; these are supporting estimates and not primary HPB values. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish and are not available in a directly applicable approved source for this specific stir-fried preparation. 4. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate for a non-starchy vegetable with similar carbohydrate and fibre profile; no direct GI measurement for stir-fried bitter gourd exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. 5. The input record listed GI 72 (high) from an 'Embedded GI Database' — this is not an approved source and has been replaced with the peer-reviewed analogous estimate. 6. The keto dietary tag from input has been removed: at 3.9 g carbohydrate/100 g the dish is low-carb but the keto classification requires full meal context and is not confirmed for this standalone dish entry.
